skill.name: mock-server
skill.description: Create mock REST, GraphQL, OpenAPI, and WireMock servers for development and testing workflows.
skill.summary: Build local mock API servers with realistic responses, errors, delays, and test integrations.
skill.author: CuriousLearner

security_audit.summary: Static analysis found many command, network, environment, and heuristic flags, but review shows they are primarily documentation examples for local mock servers. No prompt injection or confirmed malicious exfiltration was found, but the skill should publish with a warning because it instructs users to install tools, run Docker, and expose local mock services.

medium_findings.title: Executable Setup Commands in Documentation
medium_findings.description: The skill includes npm global installs, server start commands, and a Docker run command. These are legitimate for mock server setup, but users would execute third-party tools and bind local ports, so marketplace publication should include a warning.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The commands are clearly visible and relevant to the skill purpose. Risk is medium because execution is user-directed documentation, not hidden automatic behavior.

medium_findings.title: Local Mock Services Can Handle Request Data
medium_findings.description: The examples create HTTP handlers and local servers that read request bodies, headers, query parameters, and authorization headers. This is expected for mock APIs, but it can expose sensitive test data if users proxy or record real traffic without controls.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The handlers and feature list show request processing and proxy or replay capabilities. I did not find evidence that the skill itself exfiltrates data.

low_findings.title: Critical Static Heuristic Dismissed After Context Review
low_findings.description: The scanner combined code execution, network, and environment patterns into a critical heuristic. Context shows these are example snippets for local development, such as MSW development loading, localhost curl calls, and server logging.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The cited lines use localhost, relative mock routes, or benign configuration. No evidence found of external credential transmission or hidden execution.

low_findings.title: Environment Access Is Benign Configuration
low_findings.description: The process.env references check NODE_ENV before starting MSW and read PORT for a local Express server. They do not read secrets or send environment values to a remote endpoint.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: Both environment variables are non-secret configuration values in common development patterns. No evidence found of .env file access or credential harvesting.

low_findings.title: Network Calls Are Local or Mock Handler Examples
low_findings.description: HTTP and curl findings point to localhost URLs or MSW route handlers. These are normal examples for a mock server skill and are not hardcoded external collection endpoints.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The network examples use localhost or relative API paths. The risk is low because no external destination or credential payload is present.

low_findings.title: Path Traversal and Weak Crypto Flags Are False Positives
low_findings.description: The path traversal alert is a relative import used in a test setup example. The weak cryptography alerts do not correspond to cryptographic operations in the reviewed context.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The relative import stays within the example project structure, and the cited weak-crypto lines are OpenAPI or conditional response text. No evidence found of hashing or cryptographic use.

dangerous_patterns.title: Package Manager and Docker Commands
dangerous_patterns.description: The skill asks users to install packages and run containers for mock server tooling. This is expected but requires trust in third-party packages and container images.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The commands are explicit and actionable. The pattern is risky only when a user chooses to run the examples.

dangerous_patterns.title: Development Server Binding
dangerous_patterns.description: The Express and JSON Server examples bind local HTTP ports. Users should avoid exposing these mock services to untrusted networks.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The examples start local servers on configurable ports. There is no evidence of remote exposure by default.

content.value_statement: Frontend and test teams often wait for stable backend APIs. This skill helps create local mock servers with realistic responses, errors, delays, and API contract examples.
actual_capabilities: Creates JSON Server examples for simple REST CRUD mocks.
Defines MSW handlers for browser and Node test environments.
Shows Prism commands for OpenAPI-based mock servers.
Builds Express mock servers with validation, delays, errors, and authentication checks.
Provides GraphQL and WireMock examples for more complex API simulations.
Generates realistic mock data patterns with Faker.js.
limitations: The skill provides guidance and snippets, not a standalone executable server.
Users must install and trust third-party tools such as JSON Server, MSW, Prism, WireMock, and Faker.js.
Example data and tokens are placeholders and must not be used as production credentials.
Proxy and record workflows need careful filtering to avoid capturing sensitive real traffic.

use_cases.title: Unblock Frontend Development
use_cases.description: Create local REST or GraphQL mocks before backend endpoints are complete.

use_cases.title: Test Failure Handling
use_cases.description: Simulate slow responses, validation errors, network failures, and rate limits for client tests.

use_cases.title: Validate API Contracts
use_cases.description: Use OpenAPI and WireMock patterns to keep mock behavior aligned with documented contracts.

prompt_templates.title: Create a Simple REST Mock
prompt_templates.scenario: Beginner setup for a small frontend feature.
prompt_templates.prompt: Create a local mock REST API for users and posts. Include basic CRUD routes, sample data, and commands to start it.

prompt_templates.title: Add Error and Delay Scenarios
prompt_templates.prompt: Extend my mock API with validation errors, random server errors, a slow endpoint, and a timeout scenario.

prompt_templates.title: Mock From an OpenAPI Spec
prompt_templates.prompt: Use my OpenAPI description to design a Prism mock setup with dynamic examples and validation guidance.

prompt_templates.title: Design a Full Test Mock Strategy
prompt_templates.scenario: Advanced workflow across browser, Node, and integration tests.
prompt_templates.prompt: Plan a mock server strategy using MSW, Express, and WireMock. Include state reset, authentication mocks, failure modes, and safe handling of recorded traffic.

output_examples.input: I need a mock API for a user list while the backend is unavailable.
output_examples.output: A local REST mock plan with user and post resources, CRUD behavior, pagination, and start commands.

output_examples.input: My frontend must handle rate limits and network failures.
output_examples.output: A set of mock scenarios covering delayed responses, intermittent server errors, timeout behavior, and rate limit responses.

output_examples.input: We have an OpenAPI file and want realistic contract testing.
output_examples.output: A Prism-based mock workflow with validation, dynamic examples, and notes for keeping mock data synchronized.

best_practices: Keep mock responses aligned with the current API contract.
Use placeholder data and never include production secrets in mocks.
Reset mock state between tests to keep results repeatable.
anti_patterns: Do not expose local mock servers on public networks without access controls.
Do not record real API traffic that contains credentials or personal data.
Do not let mock behavior drift from production API validation rules.

faq.question: Can this skill generate a complete mock server?
faq.answer: It can guide creation of local mock servers and provide implementation patterns, but it is not a packaged server binary.

faq.question: Which mock tools does it cover?
faq.answer: It covers JSON Server, MSW, Prism, Express, Apollo GraphQL, WireMock, and Faker.js patterns.

faq.question: Is it suitable for automated tests?
faq.answer: Yes. It includes MSW and Node test setup patterns, plus reset guidance for repeatable tests.

faq.question: Does it support OpenAPI?
faq.answer: Yes. It includes Prism examples for mocking from OpenAPI specifications with validation and dynamic responses.

faq.question: Can it simulate production failures?
faq.answer: Yes. It includes delays, random errors, timeouts, rate limits, validation errors, and network errors.

faq.question: What security care is needed?
faq.answer: Use mock data, keep services local, review third-party installs, and avoid recording sensitive real traffic.
